title: Realization of waveguide many-body quantum optics
authors: Lena M. Hansen, Clara Henke, Christoph Hotter, Oliver A. D. Sandberg, Thomas Wilkens Sandø, Vasiliki Angelopoulou, Alexey Tiranov, Christoffer B. Møller, Zhe Liu, Leonardo Midolo, Nikolai Bart, Arne Ludwig, Philip Walther, Cornelis J. van Diepen, Peter Lodahl, Anders Søndberg Sørensen
abstract: Controlling light photon-by-photon is central to quantum optics. At a fundamental level, photon interactions are mediated by their coupling to atoms, and ultimate control requires deterministic light-matter interfacing of single photons to single atoms. Extending this paradigm to radiatively couple multiple individual atoms in a deterministic and scalable manner opens the arena of many-body quantum optics. Here, we realize such a setting by coherently coupling solid-state artificial atoms to a nanophotonic waveguide and demonstrate higher-order photon correlations that are controlled by the number of quantum emitters. We study the scaling of nonlinear photonic transport induced by emitter-photon scattering and demonstrate that adding a quantum emitter generates higher-order photon correlations. Specifically, we experimentally observe genuine three-photon correlations from a pair of collectively coupled emitters, while contributions from lower photon numbers are suppressed. In addition, we scale to three resonant quantum emitters coupled to the waveguide. These advancements demonstrate the onset of many-body quantum optics in waveguide quantum electrodynamics, enabling new photonic quantum simulators, the creation of many-body entangled states, and the exploration of novel quantum phase transitions.
summary: This paper demonstrates the realization of many-body quantum optics by coupling multiple quantum dots within a nanophotonic waveguide. By tuning the number of emitters, the researchers show that higher-order photon correlations can be engineered, specifically observing genuine three-photon correlations. This work provides a scalable foundation for creating many-body entangled states and quantum simulators.
main_problem: Achieving deterministic, scalable control of light-matter interactions to extend quantum optics from single-photon/single-atom regimes to many-body quantum optics.
main_result: The researchers realized many-body quantum optics in a waveguide by demonstrating that adding quantum emitters generates higher-order photon correlations, specifically observing genuine three-photon correlations from a pair of coupled emitters.
method: The experiment uses pulsed excitation of semiconductor quantum dots in a nanophotonic waveguide, measuring n-photon correlation functions via three detection channels and comparing results to theoretical simulations.
model_or_system: A nanophotonic photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) coupled to multiple InAs quantum dots (QDs) in a GaAs p-i-n diode heterostructure, where emitters are independently tunable via electrical (Stark) and magnetic (Zeeman) effects.
key_observables: n-photon correlation functions (g(2), g(3), g(4)), connected (genuine) and disconnected components of third-order correlations (Gc(3), Gd(3)), and transmission/reflection spectra.
important_parameters: Coupling phase (phi = 0.8pi), decay rate (Gamma), dephuring rate (gamma_d), beta factor, and the scaling law where the nth-order joint cumulant peaks at n = m + 1.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the impact of spectral diffusion and detector jitter can be modeled; simulations exclude blinking effects.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the waveguide schematic and simulations of photon correlations; Figure 2 displays experimental g(3) measurements for single and coupled QDs; Figure 4 shows connected vs. disconnected three-photon components; Figure 5 illustrates the scalability to three emitters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of many-body quantum optics, describes the experimental platform and tuning mechanisms, presents results for single and coupled emitters (including higher-order correlations), demonstrates scalability to three emitters, and concludes with a discussion on limitations and future directions.
why_interesting: It demonstrates the experimental onset of many-body quantum optics in a waveguide-QED setting, providing a platform for quantum simulators, many-body entanglement, and exploring novel quantum phase transitions through engineered photon-photon interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Waveguide many-body quantum optics. (A) Illustration of multiple emitters directly coupled via the waveg- uide, visualized by a connection between the emitters. The scattering of a weak coherent input pulse |α⟩in the bidi- rectional waveguide introduces a forward- and a backward-propagating field. The photon wavepackets illustrate the un- derlying concept that m coupled emitters can tailor a forward-propagating output field with enhanced m + 1-photon cor- relations and m-photon correlations in the backward direction. (B-D) Simulations of the two-time correlation function g(n)(τ) = ⟨(a†(t))n−1a†(t + τ)a(t + τ)(a(t))n−1⟩/⟨(a†(t)a(t)⟩n of the forward-propagating field. In the...

FIG. 2. Observation of strong three-photon correlations. (A-C) Waveguide scattering of a weak coherent light pulse for a single QD and (D-F) coupled QDs. (B and E) Schematic of the device, where two quantum emitters (QD1 and QD2) are electrically separated by a vertical trench indicated in the center of the device, which allows independent electrical tuning of the QDs. The scattering process in the waveguide introduces forward- and backward-propagating fields (visualized by arrows). For the interaction with a single QD; a two-level system with ground state |g⟩and excited state |e⟩, the second emitter is far-detuned from resonance (dashed line). The coupled emitter system is controlled by...

FIG. 3. Experimental two-photon correlations. Tem- porally resolved second-order correlations for (A and B) a single QD and (C and D) two coupled QDs. (A) The re- flected field for a single QD consists only of anti-correlated photons, t1 ̸= t2. (C) For coupled QDs, the reflection is generally increased, and in addition, a correlated component appears as a strong diagonal feature, t1 = t2. (B) For a sin- gle QD, the signal in the forward direction is dominated by a correlated two-photon component. (D) For coupled QDs, the signal in the forward direction is reduced, but a weak cor- related component is still observed. The coincidence counts for the QD and the QDs can be directly compared...

FIG. 4. Genuine three-photon correlations. Connected and disconnected components for the third-order correlations of the forward-propagating field for (A-D) a single QD and (E-H) a pair of coupled QDs. Normalized (A and E) connected and (B and F) disconnected components. Insets show corresponding numerical simulations. (C and G) Traces with j2 = 0 for the unnormalized correlations G(3) c (j1, j2 = 0), and G(3) d (j1, j2 = 0). Additionally, the uncorrelated components (G(1))3 are shown as a reference. The overall amplitude of the theory simulations (lines) is fitted to the experimental data (dots), assuming uncorrelated spectral diffusion. The slight discrepancy in the overall amplitude fit...

FIG. 5. Scattering from multiple emitters. (A) Visu- alization of three coupled emitters formed from a set of four QDs in a waveguide. (B) Transmission spectrum of four emit- ters within the waveguide. (C-E) Experimental data (mark- ers) and simulations (lines) of the transmission intensity for simultaneously tuning (C) single emitters, (D) pairs of emit- ters, and (E) triplets of emitters into resonance with the input field using electric and magnetic tuning. The transmission intensity is normalized to the far off-resonant detuning. The shaded areas indicate the standard error of the mean across 20 measurement runs.
